era_context: The early Italian Renaissance; Ming China under the Xuande Emperor; the Hundred Years' War in decline; the Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan territories; the Council of Constance's aftermath reshaping European Christianity.

events.title: Zheng He's seventh treasure voyage departs from Nanjing
events.description: Admiral Zheng He departs Nanjing on his seventh and final treasure voyage, commanding a massive fleet of ships (possibly 300+ vessels) carrying diplomats, merchants, and military personnel. The voyage will visit East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and Indian ports before returning in 1436. This expedition represents the apex of Chinese maritime exploration and demonstrates Ming naval supremacy. The decision to cease these voyages after 1436 reflects factional politics at court and the prioritization of land-based defense against Mongol threats, marking a turning point in Chinese foreign policy.

sources.name: Ma Huan, Ying-yai Sheng-lan (Overall Survey of the Star Raft)

events.certainty_note: The departure date of the seventh voyage is well-documented in Ming court records.

events.title: Cosimo de' Medici exiled from Florence
events.description: Cosimo de' Medici, the patriarch of the Medici banking family, is exiled from Florence by the rival Albizzi faction, which fears the Medici family's growing wealth and influence. Cosimo is banished for one year (1433–1434), during which he travels to Venice and other cities. His exile is brief but significant: it demonstrates the political instability of Florence and foreshadows the Medici family's eventual rise to dominance. Upon his return in 1434, Cosimo's position is strengthened, and he becomes the de facto ruler of Florence.

sources.name: Florentine chronicles (Vespasiano da Bisticci, Giovanni Villani)

events.certainty_note: Cosimo's exile is well-documented in Florentine records.

events.title: Hussite Wars near conclusion in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite Wars (1419–1434), a series of religious and political conflicts in Bohemia following Jan Hus's execution in 1415, are in their final phase. By 1433, the radical Taborite faction has been largely defeated by a coalition of moderate Hussites and Catholic forces. The wars represent one of the earliest major religious conflicts in Christian Europe and establish Bohemia as a center of religious reform, prefiguring the later Protestant Reformation. The conclusion of the wars in 1434–1435 marks a settlement that grants the Utraquist church recognition within the Holy Roman Empire.
key_figures: Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, King of Bohemia)
events.certainty_note: The Hussite Wars' progression is well-documented in contemporary records.

events.title: Portuguese exploration of West African coast continues
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese caravels continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1433, Portuguese traders have established contacts with Berber and sub-Saharan African merchants, initiating the Atlantic slave trade (initially small-scale) and seeking gold, spices, and geographic knowledge. These voyages represent the beginning of European maritime expansion and the eventual circumnavigation of Africa.

sources.name: Portuguese royal chronicles and navigational records
Later accounts by Gomes Eanes de Zurara (Crónica da Guiné)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1433 is attested in later chronicles; contemporary documentation is sparse.
cross_references: 1444-001 (First large-scale slave raid from West Africa)
1488-001 (Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope)

events.title: Council of Constance concludes; religious reform agenda set
events.description: The Council of Constance (1414–1418) concluded several years earlier, but its legacy continues to shape European Christianity in 1433. The council's decisions—including the condemnation of Jan Hus and the establishment of papal authority—set the agenda for religious reform that would dominate the 15th century. The Council of Florence (convened in 1434) represents a continuation of this reform effort, addressing church unity and the reconciliation of Eastern and Western Christianity.
key_figures: Pope Eugenius IV (Council of Florence, successor)
events.certainty_note: The Council of Constance's conclusions are well-documented; its influence on 1433 is indirect but significant.

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Constance (1433-005) actually concluded in 1418, not 1433; this entry describes its legacy rather than a direct event in 1433. Portuguese exploration (1433-004) is often portrayed as a unified program under Henry the Navigator, but early voyages were sporadic and motivated by diverse commercial and political interests. The Hussite Wars' dating (1433-003) is sometimes extended into 1435, and the religious settlement remained contested.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1433 is moderate. European political and military events are well-attested in chronicles and official records. Chinese court records are rich for Zheng He's voyages. Portuguese exploration is documented in later chronicles but contemporary records are sparse. African perspectives on Portuguese contact are almost entirely absent from surviving sources, reflecting the colonial bias of European documentation.

graph_edges.note: Seventh voyage departs and returns in 1436
Cosimo's exile leads to his return and rise to power
Hussite Wars near conclusion in 1433, formally conclude in 1434–1435
Council of Constance's legacy shapes the Council of Florence
